data.title: The 3 Light Bulbs
data.statement:
This puzzle is inspired by a classic logic riddle featured in The Mysterious Benedict’s Society.
You enter a room containing three switches, all initially OFF. In a different room — which you may enter only once — there are three light bulbs.
Each switch controls exactly one of the bulbs, but you don’t know which.
Your task is normally to figure out which switch controls which bulb by using clever reasoning.
But in this puzzle, the investigation has already been done for you — you are instead asked to simulate the result.
You are given:

The initial state of each bulb (0 = OFF, 1 = ON)
The current state of each switch (0 = OFF, 1 = ON)
A mapping telling you which switch controls which bulb

Your job: Determine the final state of each bulb after toggling according to the switch states.
A switch flips its corresponding bulb:

If the bulb was OFF and the switch is ON → bulb becomes ON
If the bulb was ON and the switch is ON → bulb becomes OFF
If the switch is OFF → bulb stays exactly as it was

You must output the final bulb states in order: bulb 1, bulb 2, bulb 3.
data.stubGenerator:
read b1:int b2:int b3:int
read s1:int s2:int s3:int
read m1:int m2:int m3:int
write answer
data.inputDescription:
<<Line 1:>> Three integers [[b1]] [[b2]] [[b3]] representing the initial states of the bulbs
(0 = OFF, 1 = ON).
<<Line 2:>> Three integers [[s1]] [[s2]] [[s3]] representing the states of the switches
(0 = inactive, 1 = active).
<<Line 3:>> Three integers [[m1]] [[m2]] [[m3]] describing the mapping:
switch i controls bulb [[m{{i}}]] (1 to 3).
data.outputDescription: <<Line 1:>> Three integers representing the final states of bulbs 1, 2, and 3 after applying the toggle operations.

lastVersion.statementHTML:
Goal

This puzzle is inspired by a classic logic riddle featured in The Mysterious Benedict’s Society.
You enter a room containing three switches, all initially OFF. In a different room — which you may enter only once — there are three light bulbs.
Each switch controls exactly one of the bulbs, but you don’t know which.
Your task is normally to figure out which switch controls which bulb by using clever reasoning.
But in this puzzle, the investigation has already been done for you — you are instead asked to simulate the result.
You are given:

The initial state of each bulb (0 = OFF, 1 = ON)
The current state of each switch (0 = OFF, 1 = ON)
A mapping telling you which switch controls which bulb

Your job: Determine the final state of each bulb after toggling according to the switch states.
A switch flips its corresponding bulb:

If the bulb was OFF and the switch is ON → bulb becomes ON
If the bulb was ON and the switch is ON → bulb becomes OFF
If the switch is OFF → bulb stays exactly as it was

You must output the final bulb states in order: bulb 1, bulb 2, bulb 3.
Input
Line 1: Three integers b1 b2 b3 representing the initial states of the bulbs
(0 = OFF, 1 = ON).
Line 2: Three integers s1 s2 s3 representing the states of the switches
(0 = inactive, 1 = active).
Line 3: Three integers m1 m2 m3 describing the mapping:
switch i controls bulb mi (1 to 3).
Output
Line 1: Three integers representing the final states of bulbs 1, 2, and 3 after applying the toggle operations.
Constraints
All values are integers.
0 ≤ b1, b2, b3 ≤ 1
0 ≤ s1, s2, s3 ≤ 1
m1, m2, m3 is a permutation of {1, 2, 3}.
Example
Input
0 0 0
1 0 1
1 2 3
Output
1 0 1
